author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
title: The Eleventh Breviate.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: Chapter V.—(11.)

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: The Eleventh Breviate.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: “The next question which must be put,” he says, “is, in how many ways all sin is manifested?

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: In two, if I mistake not: if either those things are done which are forbidden, or those things are not done which are commanded.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: Now, it is just as certain that all things which are forbidden are able to be avoided, as it is that all things which are commanded are able to be effected.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: For it is vain either to forbid or to enjoin that which cannot either be guarded against or accomplished.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: And how shall we deny the possibility of man’s being without sin, when we are compelled to admit that he can as well avoid all those things which are forbidden, as do all those which are commanded?”

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: My answer is, that in the Holy Scriptures there are many divine precepts, to mention the whole of which would be too laborious; but the Lord, who on earth consummated and abridged His word, expressly declared that the law and the prophets hung on two commandments, that we might understand that whatever else has been enjoined on us by God ends in these two commandments, and must be referred to them: “Thou shalt love the Lord thy God with all thy heart, and with all thy soul, and with all thy mind;” and “Thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself.”

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: “On these two commandments,” says He, “hang all the law and the prophets.”

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: Whatever, therefore, we are by God’s law forbidden, and whatever we are bidden to do, we are forbidden and bidden with the direct object of fulfilling these two commandments.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: And perhaps the general prohibition is, “Thou shalt not covet;” and the general precept, “Thou shalt love.”

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: Accordingly the Apostle Paul, in a certain place, briefly embraced the two, expressing the prohibition in these words, “Be not conformed to this world,” and the command in these, “But be ye transformed by the renewing of your mind.”

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: The former falls under the negative precept, not to covet; the latter under the positive one, to love.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: The one has reference to continence, the other to righteousness.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: The one enjoins avoidance of evil; the other, pursuit of good.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: By eschewing covetousness we put off the old man, and by showing love we put on the new.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: But no man can be continent unless God endow him with the gift; nor is God’s love shed abroad in our hearts by our own selves, but by the Holy Ghost that is given to us.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: This, however, takes place day after day in those who advance by willing, believing, and praying, and who, “forgetting those things which are behind, reach forth unto those things which are before.”

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: For the reason why the law inculcates all these precepts is, that when a man has failed in fulfilling them, he may not be swollen with pride, and so exalt himself, but may in very weariness betake himself to grace.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: Thus the law fulfils its office as “schoolmaster,” so terrifying the man as “to lead him to Christ,” to give Him his love.
